fund.name: UTI Nifty Next 50 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: This is your core, keep-it-simple fund. It tracks the Nifty Next 50 index — the 50 biggest Indian companies just outside the top 50. Think of it as buying a small slice of 50 well-known companies like Zomato, Adani Ports, Siemens India — automatically, without picking stocks yourself. The yearly fee is just 0.20%, one of the lowest possible. Over 30 years, that tiny fee difference can save you lakhs compared to actively managed funds. UTI is one of India's oldest and most trusted fund houses, and with ₹4,700+ crore in this fund, it's very stable.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.sub_category: Equity Scheme - Large & Mid Cap Fund
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund invests in both large established companies AND mid-sized growing companies. It's like owning a mix of Reliance + a company that could become the next Reliance. ICICI Prudential is one of India's most experienced fund managers, and this fund has delivered 20% yearly returns over 5 years — among the best in its category. It adds a growth kicker on top of the index fund without taking on too much extra risk. Perfect for your 30-year horizon where compounding really works its magic.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Mid-sized companies have historically grown faster than large companies in India. This index fund tracks the top 150 mid-sized companies — automatically, with zero fund manager guesswork and only a 0.20% yearly fee. At 23, you have time on your side to ride out the dips (and mid-cap stocks do dip more — sometimes 30-40% in bad years). But over 10-15 year stretches, they tend to reward patient investors well. Motilal Oswal is a well-known name in index investing in India.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Multi-Asset Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This is your shock absorber fund. It automatically spreads money across stocks, bonds (safer loans to companies), and gold — all in one fund. The fund manager decides the mix based on market conditions, so you don't have to worry about it. With ₹16,900 crore in assets, it's one of the most trusted funds in India. We're using this for your ₹5L marriage goal (6 years away) — it won't grow as fast as pure stock funds, but it won't crash as hard either. As you get closer to 2030, you can shift more here for safety.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Your emergency fund should NOT be in mutual funds — keep it in a regular savings bank account or a liquid FD. However, once your emergency fund is built (₹24,000 target if monthly expenses ~₹8,000), this overnight fund is where any extra safety money can sit. It earns around 5.5% per year — better than most savings accounts — and you can withdraw anytime within 1 working day. Think of it as a smarter savings account.

plan.setup_phase:
**Step 1 — Build your emergency fund first (Month 1–3)**
Before investing a single rupee in mutual funds, save 3 months of expenses (~₹24,000) in your regular savings bank account or a short-term FD. This is your safety net. Nothing else matters until this is done.

**Step 2 — Open a Direct Mutual Fund account (Week 1)**
Download Kuvera (kuvera.in) or Groww app. Both are free and let you invest in "Direct" plans — which means the fund house keeps more money with you instead of paying commissions. Register with your PAN card, Aadhaar, and bank account. KYC takes 10-15 minutes.

**Step 3 — Deploy your ₹50,000 existing corpus (Week 2)**
Split it as follows:
• ₹17,500 → UTI Nifty Next 50 Index Fund (one-time/lumpsum)
• ₹15,000 → ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund (one-time/lumpsum)
• ₹17,500 → ICICI Prudential Multi-Asset Fund (one-time/lumpsum — this earmarks it for your marriage goal)

**Step 4 — Set up your monthly SIPs (Week 2)**
Set up 3 auto-debit SIPs on the same date every month (pick your salary credit date + 3 days):
• ₹2,800/month → UTI Nifty Next 50 Index Fund
• ₹2,400/month → ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund
• ₹1,600/month → Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund
• ₹1,200/month → ICICI Prudential Multi-Asset Fund
Total SIP: ₹8,000/month ✅

**Step 5 — Increase SIP by 10% every April**
Every year in April (or when you get a salary hike), increase each SIP by 10%. Example: ₹8,000 becomes ₹8,800 in Year 2, ₹9,680 in Year 3, etc. This is called a "step-up SIP" and it dramatically increases your final wealth — don't skip this step.
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Check that all 4 SIPs are running and money is being deducted on time. Also confirm your emergency fund is fully built. Log in to Kuvera/Groww and you should see 4 active SIPs.

After 1 year (April): Increase each SIP by 10% as planned. Check if all funds are roughly in line with their category average — if one fund has fallen 20-30% but others haven't, don't panic, this can be normal.

After 3 years (Marriage goal check-in): You'll be 3 years from your marriage goal. Start moving your ICICI Multi-Asset Fund gains toward even safer options. If your corpus in that fund is close to ₹2.5L, you're on track.

After 5 years (Marriage fund exit): One year before the marriage target (Year 5), stop SIP in the ICICI Multi-Asset Fund and shift the balance to a bank FD or liquid fund. Don't keep marriage money in stock-heavy funds at this stage.

Every year: Check if your 4 funds are still performing comparably to similar funds. If a fund consistently lags its peers for 2+ years in a row, consider switching. Don't switch just because of 1 bad year.
After 10 years: Review your overall allocation. By then you may want to add a small-cap fund for more growth or increase SIP amounts significantly.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines:
**Once a year, do a quick check (takes 15 minutes):**

Your target split is roughly: 85% in stock funds + 15% in the hybrid/multi-asset fund.

If stocks have done really well, your stock portion might have grown to 90%+. That's fine — at your age, you can afford to let it run. Only consider rebalancing if you're 5+ years into the journey.

**For the marriage goal specifically:** Treat the ICICI Multi-Asset Fund as a separate "bucket." Don't mix it mentally with your wealth-building money. Starting in Year 4 (two years before the wedding), gradually move the marriage fund to safer options — bank FD or liquid fund — so a market crash 6 months before your wedding doesn't wipe out your plans.

**Don't over-tinker:** The biggest mistake new investors make is checking their portfolio daily and panicking when it's down. Markets drop 20-30% in bad years. That's normal. Your 30-year horizon means you will ride through at least 4-5 such crashes — and recover from all of them.
plan.rationale:
Katniss, here's why this plan is built specifically for you:

**You're 23 with 30 years ahead — that's your biggest asset.** At your age, time in the market beats everything else. Even a modest ₹8,000/month growing at 12% for 30 years becomes approximately ₹2.8 crore. With 10% annual SIP step-ups, it could grow to ₹6-8 crore. The math works in your favour — you just have to stay consistent.

**Two goals, two buckets:** Your marriage fund (6 years, ₹5L target) and your long-term wealth (30 years) need different treatment. We've kept the ICICI Multi-Asset Fund as your marriage bucket — it has stocks + bonds + gold mixed in, so it grows decently but doesn't crash as hard as a pure stock fund. Your 3 stock/index funds are purely for long-term wealth.

**Moderate risk = mostly stocks, not all stocks.** You said moderate risk. At 23, moderate doesn't mean avoiding stocks — it means not going 100% into small-cap or thematic bets. We've kept 85% in diversified stock funds and 15% in the hybrid fund, which is a healthy, sensible mix for your life stage.

**Simple and low-cost.** Two of your four funds are pure index funds with 0.20% yearly fees — among the lowest in India. These are "set and forget" — no fund manager taking big calls, just automatic investment in India's biggest companies. The other two are managed by ICICI Prudential, one of India's most experienced fund houses.

**Every rupee matters — so we kept it lean.** No complicated 6-fund portfolio. Just 4 funds, 4 SIPs, one step-up every April. That's it. You can check it quarterly and live your life otherwise.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-40% in a bad year (like 2020 or 2008). Your portfolio value on screen will fall. This is completely normal and has always recovered over 3-5 year periods. The worst thing you can do is sell in panic.

Your mid-cap index fund (20% of your money) is more volatile than the others — it can fall more during crashes. But it also grows faster over long periods. Since you're 23, you can afford to hold through the dips.

The ₹5L marriage goal in 6 years has a risk: if markets are down in Year 5-6, your multi-asset fund may not have hit ₹5L yet. This is why we'll move the marriage money to a bank FD in Year 5 — don't leave it in the market until the last moment.
Inflation risk: If you DON'T invest, ₹5L today might need to be ₹7-8L in 6 years to buy the same wedding. This plan addresses that — but locking money in your savings account does not.

Behavioural risk — the biggest risk of all: Most investors lose money not because the market crashed, but because they checked their app daily, panicked, and sold at the bottom. Commit to not selling for at least 3 years. Set the SIPs, step them up every April, and check in once a quarter — that's the whole job.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Every April, increase your total monthly SIP by 10%. In Year 1 you invest ₹8,000/month. By Year 5 that becomes ~₹11,700/month. By Year 10, ~₹18,800/month — likely still very manageable as your salary grows. This step-up alone could double your final corpus vs. keeping SIP flat forever.

aps.reasoning:
**passive_instrument_fraction (0.65):** Three of the five funds are passive index instruments — UTI Nifty Next 50 (35%), Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 (20%), and the Overnight Fund (0% effective allocation). The remaining 45% goes to actively managed funds: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap (30%) and ICICI Prudential Multi-Asset Fund (15%). Weighting by actual allocation (35% + 20% = 55% passive vs. 45% active), the passive fraction sits at approximately 0.55. However, the overnight fund is a near-passive, rules-based debt instrument and the plan's philosophy clearly leans passive, nudging this slightly higher to ~0.65.

**turnover_score (0.75):** The plan advocates annual SIP step-ups each April, once-a-year 15-minute rebalancing checks, and a specific glide path for the marriage goal bucket (shifting to FD in Year 5). There is no tactical rotation or sector-timing. The review cadence is annual or event-driven (marriage goal milestones), which is consistent with a largely buy-and-hold orientation. Minor deductions for the marriage bucket glide path and the suggestion to switch underperforming funds after 2 years of consistent lagging.

**cost_emphasis_score (0.72):** Cost minimisation is explicitly and repeatedly highlighted. The two index funds are called out at 0.20% expense ratio, described as "one of the lowest possible," with a specific mention that fee differences "can save you lakhs over 30 years." The plan also emphasises using Direct plans (no distributor commission). However, 45% of the allocation goes to actively managed funds with expense ratios of 0.95–1.10%, meaning cost is a significant but not the singular or overriding criterion — alpha expectations and goal-based logic also drive selection.

**research_vs_cost_score (0.72):** The plan explicitly avoids individual stock analysis; the index funds are "set and forget" with "no fund manager taking big calls." For the active funds, the rationale relies on fund-house reputation (ICICI Prudential's track record, AUM size) and broad category performance (5-year returns) rather than deep stock-level or sector-level analysis. This is closer to the passive/broad-market end, though it does reference historical return comparisons and category-relative performance benchmarking.

**time_horizon_alignment_score (0.85):** The plan is strongly oriented toward a 30-year compounding horizon. It repeatedly cautions against panic-selling, explicitly discourages daily monitoring, and frames short-term volatility as normal and expected. The 10% annual SIP step-up is a long-term compounding tool. The only shorter-term element is the 6-year marriage goal bucket with its glide path, which is a thoughtful but time-limited tactical overlay — hence not a perfect 1.0.

pqs.reasoning:
**Goal Alignment (0.88):** The plan does an excellent job addressing all three of Katniss's stated goals. The emergency fund is prioritized explicitly in Step 1 before any investing begins — a thoughtful, appropriate sequence. The marriage goal (₹5L in 6 years) is assigned a dedicated "bucket" via the ICICI Multi-Asset Fund, with a concrete exit strategy in Year 5. Long-term wealth building is addressed through three equity/index funds with a 30-year horizon and a step-up SIP strategy. The plan acknowledges her life stage (first job, frugal, every rupee matters) and keeps the portfolio simple and lean with just 4 funds and 4 SIPs. The step-up SIP is particularly well-aligned with her preference to "start small and increase annually." Minor deduction: the emergency fund target (₹24,000 = 3 months × ₹8,000) appears to treat investible surplus as monthly expenses, which may undercount true monthly expenses if she sends money home. Also, no mention of how the emergency fund interacts with the lumpsum deployment in Week 2 (she may not yet have the emergency fund built when deploying the ₹50,000 corpus).

**Diversification (0.65):** The plan covers equity (large/mid-cap index, large & mid-cap active, mid-cap index) and hybrid (multi-asset with equity + debt + gold), which provides reasonable asset class diversity. However, there are notable concentration issues: (1) Three out of four funds are 100% India-focused with zero international/global equity exposure — a significant geographic concentration gap for a 30-year horizon; (2) ICICI Prudential manages two of the four funds (30% + 15% = 45% of allocation), creating meaningful fund-house concentration risk; (3) All equity exposure is in large/mid-cap domestic Indian equities — no small-cap allocation and no debt-only fund for near-term goals beyond the hybrid fund. The overnight fund is listed but has 0% allocation and no lumpsum, making it essentially decorative. The plan is better than a single-fund approach but falls short of broad multi-geography, multi-asset-class diversification.

**Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.82):** For a 23-year-old with moderate risk appetite and a 30-year horizon, 85% equity / 15% hybrid is a reasonable and well-justified allocation — arguably slightly conservative (many planners would go 90-100% equity at this age), but the moderate risk label is respected. The plan correctly distinguishes between the 6-year marriage goal (needs lower volatility) and the 30-year wealth goal (can tolerate higher volatility). Projected returns (base: 12.3%, bull: 16.3%, bear: 8.3%) are realistic for a predominantly Indian equity portfolio. Mid-cap volatility is honestly disclosed. The marriage goal risk (market down in Year 5-6) is explicitly flagged with a mitigation strategy. Minor concerns: the overnight fund has a relatively high expense ratio (0.55%) for its category, which erodes its advantage over a savings account — though its 0% allocation makes this moot. The active Large & Mid Cap fund (1.1% expense ratio) is meaningfully more expensive than the index funds, which slightly dilutes long-term returns without a clear performance edge guarantee.

**Internal Consistency (0.78):** The plan is largely coherent — the rationale, allocations, SIP amounts, and lumpsum deployment all add up correctly (₹17,500 + ₹15,000 + ₹17,500 = ₹50,000 ✅; ₹2,800 + ₹2,400 + ₹1,600 + ₹1,200 = ₹8,000 ✅). The two-bucket framing (marriage vs. long-term) is maintained throughout. However, there are a few inconsistencies: (1) The overnight fund is listed as an allocation with 0% allocation, no SIP, and no lumpsum — it's a ghost entry that could confuse the investor; (2) The rebalancing guideline says "only consider rebalancing if you're 5+ years into the journey" but review checkpoints suggest active decisions at 1 and 3 years — slightly contradictory; (3) The emergency fund target is described as "₹24,000 if monthly expenses ~₹8,000" but ₹8,000 is her investible surplus, not her total expenses — this is a logical error that could leave her under-prepared; (4) The marriage goal exit strategy says "stop SIP in Year 5" but the setup phase earmarks ₹17,500 lumpsum to the multi-asset fund, while the SIP allocation of ₹1,200/month is only partially tagged to the marriage goal in the rationale but not formally separated. These are minor but real inconsistencies in an otherwise well-structured plan.
